subject: Science (CBSE)
overall_learning_objectives: Understand that children resemble their parents
Learn basic terms: heredity, trait, variation
Know that Mendel studied pea plants
Learn how boys and girls are determined
See why brothers and sisters can be different

lesson_plan.title: What is Heredity?
teaching_strategy: Ask students about their own family looks
student_activities: List 3 things they got from mother and 3 from father
Share one similarity with parents in class
Draw a simple family tree (parents + self)

lesson_plan.title: Variations – Why We Are Not Identical
teaching_strategy: Show pictures of same family or animal litter
student_activities: Observe classmates and list 5 visible differences
Discuss: how differences help in nature (e.g., animals)

lesson_plan.title: Mendel and His Pea Plants
topics_covered: Tall and short pea plants experiment
student_activities: Draw tall and short pea plants
Color dominant trait one color, recessive another
Answer: what happens when tall × short?

lesson_plan.title: How Boys and Girls Are Born
topics_covered: Mother gives X, father gives X or Y
Equal chance for boy or girl
teaching_strategy: Draw simple X and Y chromosomes on board
Show Punnett square (very basic – no complex terms)
Use analogy: father decides like tossing a coin
student_activities: Color X pink, Y blue and show combinations

lesson_plan.title: Common Inherited Traits Around Us
topics_covered: Some traits from parents, some not
Acquired traits (e.g., learning to ride bike)
teaching_strategy: Quick class survey of visible traits
student_activities: Check own earlobes/tongue rolling and note

lesson_plan.title: Fun Revision and Quiz
topics_covered: Recap all main points with pictures
teaching_strategy: Easy quiz game (hands up or group)
Discuss one fun fact (e.g., blood groups)
student_activities: Draw their favorite concept from chapter

teaching_aids: Pictures of families, pea plants, earlobes
evaluation_methods: Simple drawings and lists in notebook
teacher_tip: Keep language very simple and use lots of pictures, stories, and student examples. Focus on making students feel the chapter is about them and their families. Avoid heavy calculations; emphasize observation and fun activities.
